questions.stem: A histogram shows a bar for the class interval 20 ≤ x < 30 with a frequency density of 3.5. What is the frequency for this class?
questions.explanation: Frequency = Frequency Density × Class Width = 3.5 × 10 = 35.

questions.stem: A frequency table shows 24 values in the class 10 ≤ x < 16. What is the frequency density for this class?
questions.explanation: Class width = 16 − 10 = 6. Frequency density = Frequency / Class Width = 24 / 6 = 4.

questions.stem: A stem-and-leaf diagram has stems 3, 4, 5 with leaves: 3|1 2 5 8, 4|0 3 6 6 9, 5|2 4. How many values are greater than 45?
questions.explanation: Values greater than 45: from stem 4 — leaves 6, 6, 9 (giving 46, 46, 49) and from stem 5 — leaves 2, 4 (giving 52, 54). That is 3 + 2 = 5 values.

questions.stem: A histogram has three classes: 0 ≤ x < 5 with frequency density 2, 5 ≤ x < 15 with frequency density 3, and 15 ≤ x < 20 with frequency density 1. What is the total frequency?
questions.explanation: Frequency = frequency density × class width for each class. Class 1: 2 × 5 = 10. Class 2: 3 × 10 = 30. Class 3: 1 × 5 = 5. Total frequency = 10 + 30 + 5 = 45.

questions.stem: A data set of 11 values is displayed on a stem-and-leaf diagram with stems 1, 2, 3 and leaves: 1|3 5 9, 2|1 4 5 7 9, 3|1 4 5. What is the interquartile range of the data?
questions.explanation: The 11 ordered values are: 13, 15, 19, 21, 24, 25, 27, 29, 31, 34, 35. For 11 values, Q1 is the 3rd value = 19 and Q3 is the 9th value = 31. The interquartile range = Q3 − Q1 = 31 − 19 = 12.

questions.stem: How many ways can 4 students be chosen from a group of 9 to form a committee?
questions.explanation: This is a combination (order does not matter): ⁹C₄ = 9! / (4! × 5!) = (9 × 8 × 7 × 6) / (4 × 3 × 2 × 1) = 3024 / 24 = 126.

questions.stem: How many different 3-letter arrangements can be made from the letters of the word MATHS (no repetition)?
questions.explanation: This is a permutation (order matters): ⁵P₃ = 5! / (5−3)! = 5! / 2! = 120 / 2 = 60.

questions.stem: A team of 3 is chosen from 5 men and 4 women, with at least 1 woman required. How many possible teams are there?
questions.explanation: Total ways without restriction = ⁹C₃ = 84. Ways with NO women (all men) = ⁵C₃ = 10. Teams with at least 1 woman = 84 − 10 = 74.

questions.stem: How many different arrangements are there of all the letters in the word STATISTICS?
questions.explanation: STATISTICS has 10 letters: S appears 3 times, T appears 3 times, I appears 2 times, A appears 1 time, C appears 1 time. The number of distinct arrangements = 10! / (3! × 3! × 2!) = 3628800 / (6 × 6 × 2) = 3628800 / 72 = 50400.

questions.stem: A committee of 5 is to be chosen from 6 men and 4 women. The committee must contain at least 2 women. How many different committees can be formed?
questions.explanation: The valid cases are: (2 women, 3 men), (3 women, 2 men), or (4 women, 1 man). Case 1: ⁴C₂ × ⁶C₃ = 6 × 20 = 120. Case 2: ⁴C₃ × ⁶C₂ = 4 × 15 = 60. Case 3: ⁴C₄ × ⁶C₁ = 1 × 6 = 6. Total = 120 + 60 + 6 = 186.

questions.stem: A bag contains 3 red and 5 blue balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that both are red?
questions.explanation: P(1st red) = 3/8. Given 1st is red, P(2nd red) = 2/7. So P(both red) = (3/8) × (2/7) = 6/56 = 3/28.

questions.stem: Events A and B are such that P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.3, and A and B are independent. What is P(A ∪ B)?
questions.explanation: Since A and B are independent, P(A ∩ B) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.4 × 0.3 = 0.12. P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B) = 0.4 + 0.3 − 0.12 = 0.58.

questions.stem: A box contains 4 red balls, 3 blue balls, and 3 green balls. Two balls are drawn at random without replacement. What is the probability that both balls are the same colour?
questions.explanation: Total ways to choose 2 balls from 10: ¹⁰C₂ = 45. Ways to choose 2 of the same colour: ⁴C₂ + ³C₂ + ³C₂ = 6 + 3 + 3 = 12. P(same colour) = 12/45 = 4/15.

questions.stem: A medical test for a disease has the following properties: P(positive | disease) = 0.95 and P(positive | no disease) = 0.05. In a population, 1% of people have the disease. A randomly selected person tests positive. What is the probability that they actually have the disease? (Give your answer to 3 decimal places.)
questions.explanation: Using Bayes' theorem: P(disease | positive) = P(positive | disease) × P(disease) / P(positive). P(positive) = P(positive | disease) × P(disease) + P(positive | no disease) × P(no disease) = 0.95 × 0.01 + 0.05 × 0.99 = 0.0095 + 0.0495 = 0.0590. Therefore P(disease | positive) = 0.0095 / 0.0590 = 19/118 ≈ 0.161.

questions.stem: A discrete random variable X has the distribution: P(X=1)=0.2, P(X=2)=0.5, P(X=3)=0.3. What is E(X)?
Using the distribution in the previous question (P(X=1)=0.2, P(X=2)=0.5, P(X=3)=0.3), what is Var(X)?
If E(X) = 3 and Var(X) = 4, what is E(2X + 1)?
If E(X) = 3 and Var(X) = 4, what is Var(2X + 1)?
questions.explanation: Var(aX + b) = a²·Var(X). Therefore Var(2X + 1) = 2² × Var(X) = 4 × 4 = 16. Note that adding a constant does not affect the variance.

questions.stem: A discrete random variable X has the probability distribution P(X = x) = kx for x = 1, 2, 3, 4, and P(X = x) = 0 otherwise. Find the value of Var(X).
X ~ N(50, 16). What is the z-score corresponding to x = 54?
questions.explanation: The variance is 16, so the standard deviation σ = √16 = 4. z = (x − μ) / σ = (54 − 50) / 4 = 4 / 4 = 1.

questions.stem: X ~ N(100, 25). Using the standard normal table, P(X < 110) = Φ(2) ≈ 0.9772. What is P(X > 110)?
X ~ N(μ, 9). It is given that P(X < 17) = 0.9332, and Φ(1.5) = 0.9332. What is the value of μ?
X ~ N(μ, σ²). It is given that P(X > 20) = 0.1587 and P(X < 14) = 0.3085. Using Φ(1) = 0.8413 and Φ(0.5) = 0.6915, find the value of μ.
questions.explanation: P(X > 20) = 0.1587 means P(X < 20) = 0.8413 = Φ(1), so (20 − μ)/σ = 1. P(X < 14) = 0.3085 = 1 − 0.6915 = 1 − Φ(0.5), so (14 − μ)/σ = −0.5. From the first equation: 20 − μ = σ. From the second equation: 14 − μ = −0.5σ. Subtracting the second from the first: 6 = 1.5σ, so σ = 4. Substituting back: μ = 20 − 4 = 16.
